event: Sculptural busts of St. Haralambos and Mercury
symbols: two faces back to back — the juxtaposition of Christian sanctity and classical antiquity
description: A rough-textured clay or plaster sculpture showing two busts joined back to back. One face appears more weathered and draped, while the other shows a smoother classical profile. Greek letters are inscribed on the base. The sculpture is photographed in black and white against a plain background, emphasizing the raw, unfinished texture of the material.
theological_description: The pairing of a Christian saint (Haralambos, a third-century bishop and martyr) with the pagan figure Mercury in a Janus-like double bust evokes the complex relationship between Christianity and classical antiquity. Saint Haralambos was martyred during the persecution under Septimius Severus and is venerated in Eastern Orthodoxy as a healer and protector. The juxtaposition suggests the artist's meditation on the transition from pagan to Christian civilization — the old gods giving way to the saints, yet sharing the same sculptural form and cultural memory.

scripture_refs.reason: The catalogue of martyrs and saints who endured suffering for faith, applicable to Haralambos's martyrdom
Paul and Barnabas mistaken for Mercury and Zeus at Lystra — the tension between pagan and Christian identity
